title: Missing forceConsistentCasingInFileNames risks cross-platform build failures
reasoning: The compilerOptions section (lines 2-23) does not include `"forceConsistentCasingInFileNames": true`. This option is not part of `strict` and must be set explicitly. Without it, imports like `import X from './MyComponent'` where the actual file is named `mycomponent.tsx` will pass type-checking on case-insensitive filesystems (macOS, Windows) but fail at build time on case-sensitive filesystems (Linux CI, production deployments). Next.js production builds and Vercel deployments run on Linux, making this a concrete risk.
reproduction: On macOS, create a file `Foo.tsx` and import it as `import Foo from './foo'`. `tsc --noEmit` will succeed. The same code will fail on Linux with a module-not-found error.
recommendation: Add `"forceConsistentCasingInFileNames": true` to the `compilerOptions` block to catch casing mismatches during local development and CI.
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: No test suite is configured (detected commands show null for test). Even if tests existed, casing bugs only manifest on case-sensitive filesystems, so a macOS-only test run would not catch them.
suggestedRegressionTest: Run `tsc --noEmit` in CI on a Linux runner; with the option enabled, any existing casing mismatches in the codebase will be surfaced as compile errors.
minimumFixScope: Add a single line to tsconfig.json: `"forceConsistentCasingInFileNames": true` inside compilerOptions.
